exemplar.title: A Replication of Loftus and Palmer's 1974 Study on Memory Replication

detailedExemplarData.title: A Replication of Loftus and Palmer's 1974 Study on Memory Replication

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Aim clearly stated and relevance to legal eyewitness testimony explicitly justified.
Theory of Reconstructive Memory and Loftus & Palmer model accurately described and linked to the study.
Hypotheses correctly state and operationalise IV and DV with both research and null hypotheses articulated.
weaknesses: Minor lapse where a sentence ends abruptly, leaving an implication undeveloped.
Broader societal impact beyond courtroom settings is only briefly mentioned.
Operationalisation suggestions (e.g., more precise âverb intensityâ levels) are not fully integrated.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands were securely in the 5â6 band (aim/relevance and theory link) and the hypothesis strand fully met 5â6 descriptors. The element of minor incompleteness prevents the award of the maximum 6, placing the overall introduction performance at a solid 5.

strengths: Justification for using an independent-measures design is clear and addresses order effects and demand characteristics.
Volunteer/convenience sampling is described with basic reference to availability and ethics.
Controlled variables and materials are tabulated with an explanation of their role.
weaknesses: Sampling rationale is superficial, lacking deep justification of technique choice or bias mitigation.
Environmental controls (screen size, lighting, audio volume) are not fully detailed.
Procedure lacks necessary details on presentation equipment that could affect encoding.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the research design is well explained (band 4), the superficial treatment of sampling and missing environmental controls limit the Exploration to a lower band overall. The significant practical weaknesses justify a score of 2 out of 4.

strengths: Descriptive (mean, median, SD) and inferential (Mann-Whitney U) statistics are appropriately chosen and correctly applied.
Graphical presentation directly addresses the hypothesis with a clear bar chart and correct interpretation.
Findings are interpretable, linked back to the hypothesis, and statistical outcomes are reported accurately.
weaknesses: Effect-size reporting and confidence intervals are omitted, reducing interpretive depth.
Graph axes lack explicit units (âkm/hâ) and the chart title could be more descriptive.
Assumption checks for the Mann-Whitney U-test are not discussed and raw-data formatting has minor inconsistencies.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The analysis consistently meets many criteria for the top band but omits key elements such as effect size, confidence intervals and assumption checks. These gaps place the performance at the upper half of band 3â4, meriting a 4 out of 6.

strengths: Results are linked back to Reconstructive Memory theory, showing integration of findings with the conceptual framework.
Strengths and limitations are identified and connected to validity and reliability concerns.
Modifications are explicitly linked to limitations with clear justification for improving future reliability.
weaknesses: Alternative theoretical explanations are not considered, limiting evaluative breadth.
Some evaluation points and the conclusion are cut off or underdeveloped, reducing clarity.
Suggestions for further research and real-world implications beyond the laboratory are missing.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation shows good reference to theory and justified modifications (band 3â4 range) but lacks depth in alternative interpretations and full elaboration of points. This moderate evaluative performance corresponds to a 3 out of 6.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar chart clearly displays median perceived speeds for each condition, with labelled axes and values that speak directly to the hypothesis. The technique is appropriate and the graph is correctly interpreted, though titles and axis units could be more explicit. This fits the 5-6 band; a score of 5 is suitable.

The student states the statistical outcome (U=22.5 is significant at p<.05), rejects the null hypothesis, and explicitly links this conclusion back to the prediction that the âhitâ verb yields higher speed estimates. Findings are interpreted in relation to the collected data, fulfilling the 5-6 band. Slightly limited discussion of effect size justifies a 5 rather than 6.

The hypothesis names and operationalises the IV (critical verb: "hit" vs "bumped") and DV (perceived speed in km/h measured by a verbal question). Both research and null hypotheses are clearly stated. Operationalisation is explicit (verb wording; speed expressed in km/h), meeting all elements of the 5-6 band; precision is strong enough to merit the full 6.

Volunteer/convenience techniques are identified and basic justification provided (availability, willingness, matching school demographics). The student notes gender and language competence and briefly addresses ethical screening. While this shows some rationale, the explanation of why these methods best serve the investigation is limited. This places the work solidly in the 3-4 band; a mid-band score of 3 is appropriate.

Reconstructive Memory is described with reference to schemas and distortion, and Loftus & Palmerâs original findings are summarised to show how verb intensity could alter memory. The student then ties this theoretical explanation directly to the present investigation, arguing that verb intensity should reconstruct speed estimates. The link is explicit and accurate, satisfying the top-band requirement, though depth of theoretical debate is limited; therefore 5 rather than 6 marks are awarded.

The discussion connects the results to Reconstructive Memory theory and considers implications for eyewitness testimony, moving beyond description. However, depth is moderate and alternative interpretations are absent. This aligns with âfindings are described with reference to background theoryâ, placing it in the 3-4 band; the upper value of 4 is appropriate.

The student states a clear aim â to replicate one aspect of Loftus & Palmer (1974) to see whether post-event information (the verb "hit" vs "bumped") affects perceived speed â and then explicitly explains why this is relevant for assessing the reliability of eyewitness testimony in legal contexts. The relevance is not only identified but also elaborated upon (implications for court evidence), fulfilling the descriptor for the 5-6 band. Minor brevity in linking to broader societal impact keeps the work from a perfect 6, so a score of 5 is appropriate.

Multiple strengths and limitations are identified (design, sampling, procedural controls) and each is explained in relation to validity or reliability. The explanations are relevant and specific, meeting the criteria for the top band. Minor omissions (e.g., ethical strengths) keep it to 5 rather than 6.

Controlled variables are tabulated with both how and why they were controlled, and materials (video, laptop, consent forms) are stated and briefly justified (e.g., short clip aids retention). The explanations are present but somewhat surface-level, earning a lower 3-4-band mark of 3.

For each stated limitation, the student suggests a concrete, logically linked modification (pre-experiment to match driving schemas, excluding licensed drivers, written questionnaires to avoid misinterpretation) with clear justification of how reliability would improve. This satisfies âexplicitly linked and fully justifiedâ; the overall cogency merits 5, with slight brevity preventing a 6.

The student explains why an independent-measures design was chosen (avoid order effects and demand characteristics) and describes random allocation and single presentation of the clip. This constitutes an explanation rather than a mere description, consistent with the 3-4 band. The justification is clear and accurate; hence the upper value of 4 is assigned.

Appropriate descriptive (mean, median, mode, SD) and inferential (Mann-Whitney U with p-value, U-critical, z-score) statistics are applied correctly for an independent-measures design with ordinal/non-parametric data. Apart from a minor inconsistency in the raw-data table formatting, calculations and interpretation appear accurate, meeting the 5-6 band; a 5 is awarded because the report omits exact sample-size confirmation and effect-size reporting.

annotations.comment: The works cited list omits publication years and page numbers; adding full bibliographic details will strengthen source credibility.
The description of Bartlettâs schema theory is clear, but the student should explicitly connect how this framework underpins verb intensity influencing perceived speed in this replication.
The summary of Loftus & Palmerâs (1974) original findings effectively establishes the basis for replication, demonstrating solid comprehension of how verb intensity impacts memory retrieval.

This paragraph ends abruptly at âThis implies thatâ; the student should complete this sentence to clearly articulate the implication for eyewitness memory in the context of the current investigation.
The aim is clearly stated and its relevance to legal eyewitness testimony is succinctly justified, aligning with top-band introduction descriptors.
The Independent Variable is identified correctly, but consider operationalizing âverb intensityâ levels more precisely to strengthen construct validity.
The hypothesis is clearly articulated with both conditions specified and a directional prediction that aligns precisely with the research aim.
The null hypothesis is stated correctly, but consider rephrasing it to explicitly reference the expected numerical speed difference between conditions.
The description of volunteer and convenience sampling is superficial; explain why these techniques best serve the investigationâs goals and potential biases.
Participant demographics are noted, but there is limited explanation of how gender and language proficiency were controlled for or balanced.
Consider adding environmental controls such as screen size or room lighting in the controlled variables table to further reduce perceptual variability.
The procedure section describes steps clearly, but lacks detail on presentation equipment (e.g. screen size, audio volume) that could influence perceptual encoding.
Including participant screening for prior crash-related trauma is an ethical strength that enhances internal validity by reducing distress-induced bias.
The descriptive statistics section is thorough; to deepen analysis, include an effect size measure (e.g. r or eta squared) alongside median and standard deviation.
Referencing Fig. 1 immediately after presenting the descriptive statistics helps the reader follow the data narrative; this linkage is well executed.
The bar chartâs axes lack explicit units; add âkm/hâ to axis labels and a clear title to make the graphical presentation fully self-explanatory.
When introducing the Mann-Whitney U-test, specify any assumption checks (e.g. distribution shape) to reinforce the choice of a nonparametric method.
The inferential results are clearly reported, but including confidence intervals around the median difference would strengthen the interpretative nuance.
The concluding paragraph of inferential analysis justifies rejecting Hâ effectively; consider adding the effect size to quantify the practical significance.
The discussion effectively links findings back to reconstructive memory theory, demonstrating strong integration of results with the conceptual framework.
The discussion includes a relevant strength of the design; framing it in terms of validity could deepen the evaluation.
The evaluation lacks consideration of alternative theoretical explanations; integrating other memory models would enrich the discussion.
When proposing the schema-matching pretest, specify how matched pairs would be formed statistically to ensure clarity in future replication.
The conclusion restates the studyâs significance but would benefit from suggestions for further research to demonstrate evaluative depth.
The final limitation description is cut off mid-sentence; ensure all evaluation points are fully fleshed out in the write-up.
The procedural modification of using a paper questionnaire is well-justified and directly linked to the limitation of verbal misinterpretation.
The conclusion effectively summarizes causality but would be enhanced by reflecting on the real-world implications beyond the laboratory context.
The conclusion does not compare results with Loftus & Palmerâs original effect size; including this would contextualize the replicationâs success.
Reference formatting is inconsistent; standardize citations (e.g. APA style) and include DOIs to improve academic rigor.
